topic: A musician hears their song being performed by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The studio air hung thick and stale, smelling faintly of old coffee and dust motes dancing in the single shaft of afternoon sun. Elias leaned back in his worn leather chair, his gaze fixed on the blank screen of his laptop. He’d finished the track—a meandering melody about forgotten rain—and now he just waited. He didn't really care if anyone played it. He’d uploaded it months ago, a digital ghost in the ether. When the notification finally pinged—a strange, unfamiliar username linking to a live stream—Elias sighed, a slow, heavy expulsion of air that didn't quite reach his chest. He muted the stream, the silence rushing back in to fill the void. He picked up his lukewarm mug, the ceramic cool against his palms, and decided to start sorting through old lyric sheets instead.

[story 2]

Maya stared out the rain-streaked window of her cramped apartment, the city lights blurring into streaks of neon. Her headphones were off, resting around her neck like a discarded snake. She hadn't expected anything. Her music was just noise to her, a byproduct of late nights and cheap energy drinks. Then, the sound drifted in—a version of her latest piece, the one she’d poured three sleepless weeks into, being sung with a raw, unexpected tenderness by a stranger. Maya didn't move. Her shoulders relaxed almost imperceptibly, and she let her eyes drift toward the windowpane, watching the water run down the glass. It was just another sound in the overwhelming static of the world, nothing more.

[story 3]

For Julian, the afternoon felt stretched thin, the beige walls of his living room pressing in on him. He was scrolling through social media, mindlessly consuming content, when a familiar chord progression snagged his attention. It was his song, undeniably, but delivered by a voice entirely unfamiliar, rich and resonant. Julian didn't shift his posture; he simply stopped scrolling. His fingers stilled over the screen, and a faint, almost imperceptible tightening occurred around his jawline. He watched the performance continue for several minutes, his expression remaining utterly neutral, his breathing even. It was just an occurrence, a passing event in the relentless flow of the day, and he returned his attention to the feed as if nothing remarkable had transpired.
